schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the trespass against a neighbour: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the law of the trespass offering for wrongs done to a neighbour (vv.2-7, the speaking, God's command, the new section on the trespass against a neighbour, the occasion, after the trespass in the holy things, ch. 5, now the trespass against one's neighbour, which is also against the LORD); the LORD speaking (the trespass against a neighbour, the wrongs of fraud, robbery, deceit) — the new law (the trespass offering for sins against one's neighbour, with restitution); the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of the trespass against a neighbour introduced (the providence, God giving the law for wrongs against the neighbour, the link of the second table with the first) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the trespass against a neighbour
God giving the law of the trespass offering for wrongs done to a neighbour (vv.2-7)
the wrongs of fraud and deceit, which are also against the LORD

schools.summary: Sin against a neighbour is a trespass against the LORD: 'if a soul sin, and commit a trespass against the LORD, and lie unto his neighbour in that which was delivered him to keep, or in fellowship, or in a thing taken away by violence, or hath deceived his neighbour' — the great link — 'if a soul sin, and commit a trespass against the LORD, and lie unto his neighbour': the wrongs here against a neighbour (deceit over a deposit, fraud in partnership or fellowship, robbery by violence, oppression), yet called a 'trespass against the LORD' (the witnesses noting the significance — sin against a neighbour is sin against God, the wrong done to man is a trespass against the LORD, the second table bound to the first, the offence against the neighbour an offence against God who commands love of neighbour, the lesson — the unity of the two tables, he who wrongs his neighbour sins against God, 'inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these... ye have done it unto me,' Matthew 25:40, 45, the love of God and neighbour joined, Matthew 22:37-40; 1 John 4:20, 'he that loveth not his brother whom he hath seen, how can he love God whom he hath not seen?', the kinds of wrong, lying over a deposit, fraud in fellowship, robbery, deceit, oppression — the sins against the neighbour's goods and trust, the figure — all wrong against man a trespass against God, needing atonement and restitution); the kinds of wrong (the deposit denied, the partnership defrauded, the violent robbery, the deceit) — the sins against the neighbour's property and trust (the figure, the breach of trust and honesty, the wrong against the neighbour); sin against the neighbour a trespass against the LORD — the two tables joined (the providence, the wrong against man also against God, needing atonement and restitution, vv.4-7) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: Why is a wrong against a neighbour called a trespass against the LORD?

sides.position: Sin against a neighbour is sin against God - the wrong to man a trespass against the LORD, the second table bound to the first (Matthew 25:40, 45; 1 John 4:20), the love of God and neighbour inseparable (Matthew 22:37-40), all wrong against man needing atonement and restitution

themes: If a soul sin, and commit a trespass against the LORD, and lie unto his neighbour in that which was delivered him to keep, or in fellowship, or in a thing taken away by violence, or hath deceived his neighbour
sin against a neighbour is a trespass against the LORD (the crux of the two tables joined)
the wrong to man a trespass against God, the second table bound to the first (Matthew 25:40, 45; 1 John 4:20)
the love of God and neighbour inseparable (Matthew 22:37-40)

schools.summary: The wrong of keeping the lost and swearing falsely: 'or have found that which was lost, and lieth concerning it, and sweareth falsely; in any of all these that a man doeth, sinning therein' — among the wrongs: finding what was lost and keeping it (lying about it, denying it), and swearing falsely (perjury added to the fraud, the lost found and kept, the wrong of retaining another's lost property, the lying and false swearing, the deceit compounded by perjury, the figure, the dishonesty and the false oath, the sin against the neighbour and against God's name); the keeping of the lost and the lie (the dishonesty, the retaining of another's property, Deuteronomy 22:1-3) — the wrong (the failure to restore what was found); 'sweareth falsely; in any of all these that a man doeth, sinning therein' (the false swearing, the perjury, the lie sworn, the profaning of God's name added to the fraud, Leviticus 19:12, the figure, the dishonesty and perjury, the wrong against neighbour and against God's name, the providence, the wrongs needing restitution and atonement, vv.4-7) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Or have found that which was lost, and lieth concerning it, and sweareth falsely; in any of all these that a man doeth, sinning therein
the wrong of keeping the lost and swearing falsely
the retaining of another's property and the failure to restore it (Deuteronomy 22:1-3)
the false oath, the sin against the neighbour and against God's name (Leviticus 19:12)

schools.summary: The restoring of what was wrongfully taken: 'then it shall be, because he hath sinned, and is guilty, that he shall restore that which he took violently away, or the thing which he hath deceitfully gotten, or that which was delivered him to keep, or the lost thing which he found' — being guilty, the man to restore what he had wrongfully taken (by violence, deceit, the denied deposit, the kept lost thing, the restoring, the restitution of the wrong, the returning of what was taken, the kinds, the robbery, the fraud, the deposit, the lost thing, the figure, the restitution of the wrong, the fruit of repentance, the wrong made good, vv.5); the restoring of what was taken (the wrong made good, the property returned) — the restitution (the returning of the wrongfully gotten); 'or that which was delivered him to keep, or the lost thing which he found' (the various wrongs restored, the deposit, the lost thing, the figure, the restitution of every wrong, the wrong made good, the providence, the restoring of the wrong, the fruit of repentance, vv.5) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Then it shall be, because he hath sinned, and is guilty, that he shall restore that which he took violently away, or the thing which he hath deceitfully gotten, or that which was delivered him to keep, or the lost thing which he found
the restoring of what was wrongfully taken
the returning of what was wrongfully gotten, the wrong made good
the restitution of every wrong, the fruit of repentance (vv.5)

schools.summary: The full restitution with the fifth part to the wronged neighbour: 'or all that about which he hath sworn falsely; he shall even restore it in the principal, and shall add the fifth part more thereto, and give it unto him to whom it appertaineth, in the day of his trespass offering' — the man restoring the principal (the full amount) and adding a fifth part (twenty per cent), giving it to the wronged neighbour, on the day he brought his trespass offering (the principal restored, the full wrong made good, the fifth part added, the restitution with increase, more than the bare wrong, the to whom it appertaineth, restored to the very person wronged, not merely to God, the day of his trespass offering, the restitution joined with the atonement, both made together); the full amends — the principal with the added fifth (the witnesses noting the lesson — restitution to the wronged neighbour as part of repentance, the wrong against man made good to man, restored with increase, before or with the offering to God, the restitution and the atonement joined, Matthew 5:23-24, 'first be reconciled to thy brother, and then come and offer thy gift'; Luke 19:8, Zacchaeus' restitution, the necessity of making the wrong good, no true repentance or acceptable offering while the wrong to the neighbour stands unrestored); the restitution to the very person wronged, on the day of the offering (the restitution and atonement together) — the providence (the wrong made good to the neighbour, joined with the atonement to God) — Clarke, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the restitution to the neighbour with the offering teach?

sides.position: Restitution to the wronged neighbour as part of true repentance - the wrong made good to man with increase (the principal plus a fifth), joined with the atonement to God on the day of the offering (Matthew 5:23-24; Luke 19:8); no acceptable offering while the wrong to the neighbour stands unrestored

themes: He shall even restore it in the principal, and shall add the fifth part more thereto, and give it unto him to whom it appertaineth, in the day of his trespass offering
the full restitution with the fifth part to the wronged neighbour
the wrong made good to the very neighbour, restored with increase, the fruit of repentance
joined with the atonement to God, no acceptable offering while the wrong stands (Matthew 5:23-24; Luke 19:8)

schools.summary: The ram brought for the trespass against a neighbour: 'and he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD, a ram without blemish out of the flock, with thy estimation, for a trespass offering, unto the priest' — after restitution to the neighbour, the man bringing a ram (with the valuation) for a trespass offering to the LORD (the ram, the costly victim, as for the holy things, Leviticus 5:15, the to the LORD, the offering to God, after the restitution to man, the order, first the restitution to the neighbour, then the atonement to God, the figure, the unblemished ram, Christ, the atonement to God after the wrong made good to man); the trespass offering to the LORD (the atonement to God after the restitution, vv.5) — the order (restitution first, then atonement); 'a ram without blemish out of the flock, with thy estimation' (the unblemished ram with the valuation, the costly victim, Leviticus 5:15, the figure, the unblemished ram, Christ, the satisfaction to God, the providence, the ram brought, the atonement to God after the wrong made good) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD, a ram without blemish out of the flock, with thy estimation, for a trespass offering, unto the priest
the ram brought for the trespass against a neighbour
the costly ram, the atonement to God after the wrong made good to man (Leviticus 5:15)
first the restitution to the neighbour, then the atonement to God, the figure of Christ

schools.summary: The atonement and forgiveness for the trespass against a neighbour: 'and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD: and it shall be forgiven him for any thing of all that he hath done in trespassing therein' — the priest making atonement, and the sin being forgiven, for all the wrongs the man had done (the atonement, the covering of sin by the blood, after the restitution, the forgiveness, the sin forgiven, the wrong against neighbour and God pardoned, the for any thing of all, the full forgiveness for all the trespasses, the figure, the atonement and forgiveness through Christ, the wrong against man and God pardoned, Ephesians 1:7; Colossians 2:13); the atonement before the LORD (the covering of sin, after the restitution) — the figure (the atonement of Christ, the one sacrifice, Hebrews 9:22); 'it shall be forgiven him for any thing of all that he hath done in trespassing therein' (the full forgiveness for all the wrongs, the sin pardoned, the wrong against neighbour and God forgiven, the figure, the full forgiveness through Christ, Ephesians 1:7, the providence, the atonement and forgiveness for the trespass against the neighbour) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD: and it shall be forgiven him for any thing of all that he hath done in trespassing therein
the atonement and forgiveness for the trespass against a neighbour
the atonement by the blood and the full forgiveness for all the wrongs
the wrong against neighbour and God pardoned through Christ (Hebrews 9:22; Ephesians 1:7)

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the law of the burnt offering for the priests: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the priests' instructions (the law) for the offerings — the burnt offering (vv.9-13), the meal offering (vv.14-23), the sin offering (vv.24-30, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the priests' duties in the offerings, the occasion, after the laws of the offerings for the people, chs. 1-6, now the priests' charge in handling them); the LORD speaking (the priests' law of the offerings, the charge to Aaron and his sons) — the new section (the priests' duties, the law of each offering for those who minister); the LORD spake unto Moses — the priests' law of the offerings introduced (the providence, God giving the priests their charge in the offerings, the orderly service of God's house) — Henry, Gill, Keil, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the law of the burnt offering for the priests

schools.summary: The law of the burnt offering burning all night: 'command Aaron and his sons, saying, This is the law of the burnt offering: It is the burnt offering, because of the burning upon the altar all night unto the morning, and the fire of the altar shall be burning in it' — the law of the burnt offering: it to burn on the altar all night until morning, the fire kept burning (the burnt offering, the whole offering, wholly consumed, the continual offering, the evening offering burning through the night, the all night, the offering burning continually, the worship ceaseless, the fire kept burning, the altar fire never allowed to die, vv.13); the burnt offering burning all night (the continual offering, the daily evening sacrifice burning through the night, Exodus 29:38-42) — the figure (the continual offering, the perpetual devotion, Christ's offering of perpetual efficacy, Hebrews 10:14, the ceaseless worship); 'the fire of the altar shall be burning in it' (the fire kept burning, the altar fire never out, the offering kept burning, vv.13, the figure, the perpetual fire, the continual devotion, the unceasing worship, the providence, the burnt offering and the fire continual, the figure of perpetual offering and devotion) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley, Calvin.

a figure of perpetual offering and ceaseless devotion (Hebrews 10:14)

schools.summary: The priest in linen taking up the ashes: 'and the priest shall put on his linen garment, and his linen breeches shall he put upon his flesh, and take up the ashes which the fire hath consumed with the burnt offering on the altar, and he shall put them beside the altar' — the priest, clothed in his linen garments, taking up the ashes of the burnt offering and putting them beside the altar (the linen garments, the holy vestments, the purity of the priest, the linen of righteousness, the ashes, the remains of the consumed offering, the proof of the accepted sacrifice, the taking up, the daily clearing of the ashes, the figure, the priest in linen, the purity, the ashes, the evidence of the finished offering); the linen garments (the holy vestments, the purity of the ministering priest, Exodus 28:42) — the figure (the white linen, the righteousness, Revelation 19:8); 'take up the ashes which the fire hath consumed with the burnt offering... and he shall put them beside the altar' (the ashes taken up, the remains of the offering, the proof of the accepted sacrifice, the figure, the ashes, the evidence of the finished and accepted offering, the sacrifice consumed, the providence, the daily care of the altar, the ashes removed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall put on his linen garment, and his linen breeches shall he put upon his flesh, and take up the ashes which the fire hath consumed with the burnt offering on the altar, and he shall put them beside the altar

the ashes the evidence of the accepted, finished sacrifice

schools.summary: The carrying out of the ashes to a clean place: 'and he shall put off his garments, and put on other garments, and carry forth the ashes without the camp unto a clean place' — the priest changing his garments (out of the holy linen, into other clothes) and carrying the ashes outside the camp to a clean place (the changing of garments, the holy garments not worn outside the sanctuary, the distinction of the holy from the common, the carrying out, the ashes removed to a clean place outside the camp, the clean place, the ashes, though removed, kept in a clean place, honored even in their removal, the figure, the distinction of holy and common, the reverent care even of the ashes of the offering); the changing of garments (the holy linen not worn in the common task, the distinction of the sacred, Ezekiel 44:19) — the reverence (the holy garments kept holy); 'carry forth the ashes without the camp unto a clean place' (the ashes carried out to a clean place, the reverent disposal, the figure, the remains of the offering honored, kept clean, the providence, the orderly, reverent care of the altar and its ashes) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The fire kept burning and fed every morning: 'and the fire upon the altar shall be burning in it; it shall not be put out: and the priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and lay the burnt offering in order upon it; and he shall burn thereon the fat of the peace offerings' — the fire on the altar to be kept always burning, never put out, the priest feeding it with wood every morning and laying the burnt offering on it (the fire kept burning, never extinguished, vv.13, the wood every morning, the daily feeding of the fire, the burnt offering laid in order, the daily morning offering, Exodus 29:38-39, the fat of the peace offerings, the fat burned, the best given to God); the fire never put out (the perpetual fire, vv.13) — the figure (the continual worship, the unceasing devotion, the fire of the Spirit, never to be quenched, 1 Thessalonians 5:19); 'the priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and lay the burnt offering in order upon it' (the daily care, the fire fed, the morning offering, Exodus 29:38-39, the figure, the daily renewal of devotion, the continual offering and feeding of the fire, the providence, the perpetual fire kept and fed, the continual worship) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the fire upon the altar shall be burning in it; it shall not be put out: and the priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and lay the burnt offering in order upon it
the fire kept burning and fed every morning
the perpetual fire kept and daily fed, the morning offering (Exodus 29:38-39)
the figure of continual worship and the unquenched fire of devotion (1 Thessalonians 5:19)

schools.summary: The perpetual fire that shall never go out: 'the fire shall ever be burning upon the altar; it shall never go out' — the command — 'the fire shall ever be burning upon the altar; it shall never go out': the fire on the altar to burn perpetually, never to be allowed to go out, day and night, continually (the witnesses noting the significance — the perpetual fire, the unceasing fire, kept always burning, never extinguished, the lesson — the figure of continual worship and devotion, the fire that never goes out, the ceaseless offering, the perpetual devotion of God's people, the worship that never ceases, and the fire originally kindled from heaven, Leviticus 9:24, the fire that came out from the LORD and consumed the offering, kept burning ever after, the divine origin of the altar fire, not to be replaced by strange fire, Leviticus 10:1, the figure — the continual offering of Christ's perpetual efficacy, Hebrews 10:12-14, the unceasing intercession, Hebrews 7:25, and the fire of the Spirit and of devotion never to be quenched in the believer's heart, 'quench not the Spirit,' 1 Thessalonians 5:19, the fervent spirit, Romans 12:11, the fire on the altar of the heart, the love of God never to grow cold, Revelation 2:4); the fire never to go out (the perpetual, ceaseless fire, kept day and night) — the figure (the continual devotion, the unceasing worship, the fire of love never to be quenched, the lesson, the warning against letting the fire of devotion die, the continual feeding of the flame); the ever-burning fire — the unceasing worship (the providence, the perpetual fire, the figure of continual offering, intercession, and devotion) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the perpetual fire signify?

sides.position: The perpetual fire, kept always burning (originally kindled from heaven, Leviticus 9:24) - a figure of continual worship and devotion, of Christ's offering of perpetual efficacy and unceasing intercession (Hebrews 10:12-14; 7:25), and of the fire of the Spirit and love never to be quenched in the believer's heart (1 Thessalonians 5:19; Romans 12:11)

themes: The fire shall ever be burning upon the altar; it shall never go out
the perpetual fire that shall never go out (the crux of the unceasing fire)
the perpetual fire, originally kindled from heaven (Leviticus 9:24)
a figure of continual worship, Christ's perpetual efficacy, and the fire of the Spirit never to be quenched (Hebrews 10:12-14; 1 Thessalonians 5:19)

schools.summary: The law of the meal offering: 'and this is the law of the meat offering: the sons of Aaron shall offer it before the LORD, before the altar' — the law of the meal (meat) offering: the priests offering it before the LORD, before the altar (the meal offering, the offering of fine flour, oil, and frankincense, Leviticus 2:1, the priests offering it, the sons of Aaron presenting it before the LORD, the before the altar, the place of presentation, the figure, the meal offering, the figure of Christ's perfect humanity and the believer's devotion and thanksgiving, the fruit of the ground offered to God); the meal offering (the offering of fine flour and oil, Leviticus 2:1) — the figure (the perfect humanity of Christ, fine flour without leaven, the believer's devotion and thanksgiving); 'the sons of Aaron shall offer it before the LORD, before the altar' (the priests offering it before the LORD, the presentation before God, the providence, the meal offering presented, the figure of devotion offered to God, the figure, the offering of the fruit of the ground, the thankful devotion) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And this is the law of the meat offering: the sons of Aaron shall offer it before the LORD, before the altar

schools.summary: The handful and frankincense burned as a memorial: 'and he shall take of it his handful, of the flour of the meat offering, and of the oil thereof, and all the frankincense which is upon the meat offering, and shall burn it upon the altar for a sweet savour, even the memorial of it, unto the LORD' — the priest taking a handful of the flour and oil, with all the frankincense, and burning it on the altar for a sweet savour, as the memorial (the handful, the memorial portion, Leviticus 2:2, the all the frankincense, all the frankincense burned, the whole fragrance to God, the sweet savour, the pleasing offering, the acceptance, the memorial, the portion presented to God, bringing the offering to remembrance, the figure, the sweet savour of Christ's offering, Ephesians 5:2, the memorial of devotion); the handful and all the frankincense (the memorial portion, Leviticus 2:2) — the figure (the memorial, the sweet savour, the acceptance); 'shall burn it upon the altar for a sweet savour, even the memorial of it, unto the LORD' (the memorial burned for a sweet savour, the pleasing offering, the acceptance before God, the figure, the sweet savour of Christ, Ephesians 5:2, the devotion accepted, the providence, the memorial burned, the rest to the priests, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he shall take of it his handful, of the flour of the meat offering, and of the oil thereof, and all the frankincense which is upon the meat offering, and shall burn it upon the altar for a sweet savour, even the memorial of it, unto the LORD
the handful and frankincense burned as a memorial
the memorial portion burned for a sweet savour, the offering accepted (Leviticus 2:2)
the figure of the sweet savour of Christ (Ephesians 5:2)

schools.summary: The priests eating the remainder of the meal offering: 'and the remainder thereof shall Aaron and his sons eat: with unleavened bread shall it be eaten in the holy place; in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation they shall eat it' — the rest of the meal offering (after the memorial was burned) eaten by the priests, unleavened, in the holy place (the priests' portion, the remainder, the priests fed from the offering, the altar their living, 1 Corinthians 9:13, the unleavened, no leaven, the purity, the sincerity, 1 Corinthians 5:8, the holy place, eaten in the sacred precinct, the holiness of the food); the priests fed from the offering (the altar their portion, the maintenance of the ministry, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, 'they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple') — the provision (the priests living of the altar, the figure of the ministry sustained by the gospel); 'with unleavened bread shall it be eaten in the holy place' (the unleavened bread, eaten in the holy place, the purity, the holiness of the food, the sacredness of the priests' portion, the figure, the unleavened sincerity, 1 Corinthians 5:8, the holy food in the holy place, the providence, the priests' portion, holy, eaten in the holy place) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

schools.summary: The offering unleavened and most holy: 'it shall not be baken with leaven. I have given it unto them for their portion of my offerings made by fire; it is most holy, as is the sin offering, and as the trespass offering' — the meal offering not to be baked with leaven, given to the priests as their portion, and it being most holy (like the sin and trespass offerings, the no leaven, the purity, no corruption, 1 Corinthians 5:8, the portion, the priests' share of the offerings, the most holy, the highest degree of holiness, like the sin and trespass offerings, to be eaten only by the priests in the holy place); the priests' portion (their share of the offerings, the ministry's living, vv.16) — the provision (the priests fed from God's offerings); 'it is most holy, as is the sin offering, and as the trespass offering' (the most holy, the highest holiness, the offering sacred, like the sin and trespass offerings, the figure, the holiness of God's things, the sacredness of the offering, the providence, the offering most holy, the priests' portion, the holiness guarded) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The holiness communicated by the offering: 'all the males among the children of Aaron shall eat of it. It shall be a statute for ever in your generations concerning the offerings of the LORD made by fire: every one that toucheth them shall be holy' — the communicated holiness — 'every one that toucheth them shall be holy': the offerings so holy that whatever (or whoever) touched them became holy (consecrated, set apart, required to be holy, the witnesses noting the significance — the holiness of the offerings communicated by contact, the most holy things imparting their holiness, requiring holiness in whoever touched them, only the consecrated priests permitted to touch and eat, the lesson — the contagious holiness of the sacred, the holy things consecrating what touches them, the sacredness not to be profaned, the holiness imparted, Exodus 29:37; 30:29, the debate — whether 'shall be holy' means the thing becomes holy, consecrated, withdrawn from common use, or that only the holy may touch it, the witnesses generally holding the holiness is imparted, the touch consecrating, the most holy things communicating holiness, the figure — the holiness of God's things, and ultimately the holiness of Christ and his offering that sanctifies all who touch him by faith, the woman touching his garment, Mark 5:27-30, the holiness that flows from him, Hebrews 10:10, 'we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ'); the priests (the males of Aaron's line) eating the offering (the consecrated alone partaking) — the holiness (only the consecrated permitted, the sacred food); the holiness communicated, a statute for ever — the perpetual sacredness (the providence, the holiness of the offerings, the consecration imparted, the figure of sanctification through Christ) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'every one that toucheth them shall be holy' mean?

sides.position: The most holy offerings imparting their holiness by contact (Exodus 29:37; 30:29) - the contagious holiness of the sacred (whatever touches becomes consecrated, only the holy permitted), a figure of the holiness of Christ and his offering that sanctifies all who touch him by faith (Mark 5:27-30; Hebrews 10:10)

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the high priest's offering: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the law of the high priest's daily meal offering (vv.20-23, the speaking, God's command, the high priest's offering, the occasion, after the law of the meal offering for the people, now the high priest's own continual offering); the LORD speaking (the high priest's offering, the daily offering of the anointed priest) — the new law (the high priest's continual meal offering); the LORD spake unto Moses — the high priest's offering introduced (the providence, God giving the law of the high priest's daily offering, the continual devotion of the priest) — Gill, Ellicott.

schools.summary: The high priest's perpetual daily offering: 'this is the offering of Aaron and of his sons, which they shall offer unto the LORD in the day when he is anointed; the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a meat offering perpetual, half of it in the morning, and half thereof at night' — the high priest offering a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a perpetual (daily) meal offering, half in the morning and half at night, from the day of his anointing (the perpetual offering, the daily, continual offering of the high priest, the morning and night, the offering divided, the continual devotion morning and evening, the figure, the high priest's continual offering, the perpetual devotion, Christ the High Priest's perpetual offering and intercession, Hebrews 7:25, 27); the perpetual daily offering (the high priest's continual offering, from his anointing) — the figure (the continual devotion, Christ's perpetual offering, Hebrews 7:27, 'who needeth not daily... for this he did once'); 'half of it in the morning, and half thereof at night' (the offering morning and evening, the continual devotion, the daily worship, the figure, the morning and evening sacrifice, the perpetual devotion, Psalm 55:17, the providence, the high priest's continual offering, the figure of perpetual devotion and Christ's offering) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: This is the offering of Aaron and of his sons... the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a meat offering perpetual, half of it in the morning, and half thereof at night
the high priest's perpetual daily offering
the high priest's continual daily offering, morning and evening (Psalm 55:17)
the figure of perpetual devotion and Christ the High Priest's offering (Hebrews 7:25, 27)

schools.summary: The high priest's offering baked with oil for a sweet savour: 'in a pan it shall be made with oil; and when it is baken, thou shalt bring it in: and the baken pieces of the meat offering shalt thou offer for a sweet savour unto the LORD' — the high priest's meal offering made in a pan with oil, baked, and offered in pieces for a sweet savour (the pan and oil, the preparation, the oil of the Spirit mingled with the offering, the baked, the offering prepared by fire, the sweet savour, the pleasing offering, the acceptance, Ephesians 5:2, the figure, the offering mingled with oil, the Spirit, the sweet savour of devotion and of Christ); the offering made with oil (the oil mingled, the figure of the Spirit, Leviticus 2:5) — the figure (the offering anointed with the Spirit); 'the baken pieces of the meat offering shalt thou offer for a sweet savour unto the LORD' (the offering for a sweet savour, the pleasing devotion, the acceptance, the figure, the sweet savour of Christ, Ephesians 5:2, the devotion accepted, the providence, the high priest's offering, the sweet savour of perpetual devotion) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: In a pan it shall be made with oil; and when it is baken, thou shalt bring it in: and the baken pieces of the meat offering shalt thou offer for a sweet savour unto the LORD
the high priest's offering baked with oil for a sweet savour
the offering mingled with oil, the figure of the Spirit (Leviticus 2:5)
offered for a sweet savour, the figure of Christ (Ephesians 5:2)

schools.summary: The high priest's offering wholly burnt: 'and the priest of his sons that is anointed in his stead shall offer it: it is a statute for ever unto the LORD; it shall be wholly burnt' — the succeeding high priest (anointed in his stead) offering it, a perpetual statute, and it being wholly burnt (not eaten, unlike the people's meal offering, the succession, each high priest in turn offering it, the perpetual office, the wholly burnt, the high priest's offering wholly consumed, none eaten, the whole given to God, the difference, the priest could not eat his own offering, the whole burnt, the figure, the whole devotion of the high priest given to God, Christ wholly given); the succeeding high priest offering it (the perpetual office, each in turn) — the succession (the continual office of the high priest, until Christ the abiding High Priest, Hebrews 7:23-24); 'it shall be wholly burnt' (the whole burnt, none eaten, the whole given to God, the reason, the priest not eating his own offering, the whole devotion to God, the figure, the whole devotion, Christ wholly given, Ephesians 5:2, the providence, the high priest's offering wholly burnt, the whole given to God) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the priest of his sons that is anointed in his stead shall offer it: it is a statute for ever unto the LORD; it shall be wholly burnt
the high priest's offering wholly burnt
the high priest's offering wholly given to God, none eaten
the figure of the whole devotion and of Christ wholly given (Ephesians 5:2; Hebrews 7:23-24)

schools.summary: That the priest's own offering is wholly burnt: 'for every meat offering for the priest shall be wholly burnt: it shall not be eaten' — every meal offering made for (by) the priest himself wholly burnt, not eaten (the rule, the priest's own offering wholly given to God, none eaten, the reason, the priest could not eat his own offering, as he ate the people's, lest he profit from his own sin offering, the whole given to God, the figure, the priest's whole devotion, none kept back, the contrast with the people's offering, eaten by the priests); the priest's offering wholly burnt (none eaten, the whole to God) — the reason (the priest not benefiting from his own offering); 'it shall not be eaten' (none eaten, the whole given to God, the priest's whole devotion, the figure, the whole devotion, none kept back, the providence, the priest's offering wholly burnt, the whole to God) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: For every meat offering for the priest shall be wholly burnt: it shall not be eaten
that the priest's own offering is wholly burnt
the priest's own offering wholly given to God, none eaten
the figure of the whole devotion, none kept back

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the law of the sin offering: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the priests' law of the sin offering (vv.25-30, the speaking, God's command, the law of the sin offering for the priests, the occasion, after the burnt and meal offerings, now the priests' charge in the sin offering); the LORD speaking (the law of the sin offering, the priests' duties) — the new section (the priests' charge in the sin offering, its holiness and disposal); the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of the sin offering introduced (the providence, God giving the priests their charge in the sin offering, the holiness of the offering) — Henry, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the law of the sin offering
God giving the priests their charge in the sin offering (vv.25-30)
the holiness of the offering

schools.summary: The sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, most holy: 'speak unto Aaron and to his sons, saying, This is the law of the sin offering: In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed before the LORD: it is most holy' — the sin offering killed in the same place as the burnt offering (the north side of the altar, Leviticus 1:11), and it being most holy (the place, the same as the burnt offering, the appointed place of sacrifice, the killing before the LORD, the death of the victim, the atonement, the most holy, the sin offering of the highest holiness); the sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering (the same holy place, Leviticus 1:11; 4:24) — the figure (the death of the victim, the atonement, Christ slain); 'it is most holy' (the sin offering most holy, the highest holiness, the offering for sin sacred, the witnesses noting the paradox — the offering that bears sin is itself most holy, the sin-bearer not defiled but most holy, the figure of Christ made sin yet holy, harmless, undefiled, Hebrews 7:26; 2 Corinthians 5:21, the providence, the sin offering most holy, the figure of the holy sin-bearer) — Calvin, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: How is the sin offering 'most holy' though it bears sin?

sides.position: The paradox of the sin-bearer itself most holy - the offering that bears sin not defiled but most holy, a figure of Christ made sin for us yet holy, harmless, undefiled (Hebrews 7:26; 2 Corinthians 5:21)

themes: This is the law of the sin offering: In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed before the LORD: it is most holy
the sin offering killed in the place of the burnt offering, most holy
slain in the appointed place of sacrifice (Leviticus 1:11; 4:24)
most holy though bearing sin, the figure of Christ made sin yet holy and undefiled (Hebrews 7:26; 2 Corinthians 5:21)

schools.summary: The priest eating the sin offering in the holy place: 'the priest that offereth it for sin shall eat it: in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation' — the priest who offered the sin offering eating it (those whose blood was not brought into the holy place, vv.30), in the holy place (the eating, the priest partaking of the sin offering, bearing the sin of the people, sharing in the atonement, the holy place, eaten in the sacred court, the figure, the priest bearing the iniquity of the people, eating the sin offering, Leviticus 10:17, 'God hath given it you to bear the iniquity of the congregation,' the priest's participation in the atonement, a figure of Christ who bore our sins); the priest eating the sin offering (bearing the people's sin, Leviticus 10:17) — the figure (the priest bearing the iniquity, sharing in the atonement, Christ bearing our sins); 'in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation' (the sin offering eaten in the holy place, the holiness of the food, the sacred eating, the figure, the holy participation in the atonement, the providence, the priest eating the sin offering in the holy place, bearing the iniquity) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: The priest that offereth it for sin shall eat it: in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation
the priest eating the sin offering in the holy place
the priest bearing the iniquity of the people, sharing in the atonement (Leviticus 10:17)
a figure of Christ who bore our sins

schools.summary: The holiness communicated by the sin offering's flesh and blood: 'whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy: and when there is sprinkled of the blood thereof upon any garment, thou shalt wash that whereon it was sprinkled in the holy place' — the communicated holiness — 'whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy': whatever touched the flesh of the most holy sin offering became holy (consecrated, set apart, the witnesses noting the significance — the holiness communicated by the sin offering, the most holy flesh imparting holiness to whatever touched it, as the meal offering, vv.18, the lesson — the contagious holiness of the sin offering, the holiness imparted by contact, the sacredness not to be profaned, and the sprinkled blood, the garment sprinkled with the blood to be washed in the holy place, the blood so holy it consecrated and required washing in the sacred place, the figure — the holiness of the sin offering, and of Christ the true sin offering, whose holiness sanctifies and whose blood cleanses, the blood of Christ that sanctifies and cleanses, Hebrews 9:13-14; 13:12; 1 John 1:7, the holiness that flows from him to all who touch him by faith); the blood-sprinkled garment washed in the holy place (the holiness of the blood, the sacred care, the blood not to be treated commonly) — the figure (the holiness and cleansing power of the blood, the blood of Christ honored and cleansing); the flesh consecrating, the blood cleansing — the holiness imparted (the providence, the holiness of the sin offering, the figure of Christ's sanctifying holiness and cleansing blood) — Wesley, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does the holiness of the sin offering's flesh and blood signify?

sides.position: The most holy flesh imparting holiness by contact (as vv.18), the blood so holy it required washing in the sacred place - a figure of the holiness and cleansing of Christ the true sin offering, whose holiness sanctifies and whose blood cleanses (Hebrews 9:13-14; 13:12; 1 John 1:7)

themes: Whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy: and when there is sprinkled of the blood thereof upon any garment, thou shalt wash that whereon it was sprinkled in the holy place
the holiness communicated by the sin offering's flesh and blood
the most holy flesh consecrating and the blood requiring sacred washing
a figure of Christ whose holiness sanctifies and whose blood cleanses (Hebrews 9:13-14; 13:12; 1 John 1:7)

schools.summary: The cleansing of the vessels of the sin offering: 'but the earthen vessel wherein it is sodden shall be broken: and if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water' — the earthen vessel in which the sin offering was boiled to be broken, and a brazen pot scoured and rinsed (the earthen vessel broken, the porous earthen, absorbing the holy, broken, not used again, the brazen pot scoured, the harder brass cleansed and rinsed, the reason, the holiness absorbed by the vessel, the sacredness not to be carried into common use, the figure, the holiness of the sin offering, the care that the sacred not be profaned, the earthen vessel, frail, broken, the figure of the body, 2 Corinthians 4:7); the earthen vessel broken (the porous earthen absorbing the holy, not reused) — the holiness (the sacred not to be carried into common use); 'if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water' (the brazen pot scoured and rinsed, the harder metal cleansed, not absorbing as the earthen, the figure, the cleansing of the vessel, the care for holiness, the providence, the vessels cleansed or broken, the holiness guarded) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: But the earthen vessel wherein it is sodden shall be broken: and if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water
the cleansing of the vessels of the sin offering
the vessels broken or cleansed, the holiness of the sin offering guarded
the sacred not carried into common use

schools.summary: The priests eating the most holy sin offering: 'all the males among the priests shall eat thereof: it is most holy' — all the male priests eating of the sin offering (those whose blood was not brought into the holy place, vv.30), for it was most holy (the priests eating, the male priests partaking, bearing the iniquity, vv.26, the most holy, the highest holiness, eaten only by the consecrated priests, in the holy place, the figure, the priests bearing the iniquity, sharing in the atonement, the most holy offering for the consecrated); the male priests eating (the consecrated alone, bearing the sin, vv.26) — the figure (the priests bearing the iniquity of the people); 'it is most holy' (the sin offering most holy, the highest holiness, for the consecrated priests only, the figure, the holiness of the offering, the sacred food, the providence, the sin offering most holy, the priests' sacred portion) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: All the males among the priests shall eat thereof: it is most holy
the priests eating the most holy sin offering
the consecrated priests eating the most holy sin offering, bearing the iniquity (vv.26)
sharing in the atonement, the sacred portion

schools.summary: The sin offering whose blood was brought in being wholly burnt: 'and no sin offering, whereof any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle of the congregation to reconcile withal in the holy place, shall be eaten: it shall be burnt in the fire' — the sin offerings whose blood was brought into the holy place (for the priest and the congregation, ch. 4) not eaten but wholly burnt (without the camp, the distinction, the sin offerings whose blood went within were burnt, not eaten, those whose blood stayed without were eaten by the priests, the reconciliation, the blood brought in to reconcile, to make atonement in the holy place, the burning, the body wholly burnt, as Leviticus 4:12); the blood brought in to reconcile (the atonement made in the holy place, the higher offerings) — the figure (the blood of reconciliation, Christ's blood brought into the true holy place, Hebrews 9:12, the reconciliation by the blood, 2 Corinthians 5:18-19; Colossians 1:20); 'it shall be burnt in the fire' (the body wholly burnt, not eaten, the higher sin offerings burnt without the camp, Leviticus 4:12, the figure, the figure of Christ suffering without the gate, Hebrews 13:11-13, the providence, the distinction of the sin offerings, the highest burnt, the reconciliation by the blood) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And no sin offering, whereof any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle of the congregation to reconcile withal in the holy place, shall be eaten: it shall be burnt in the fire
the sin offering whose blood was brought in being wholly burnt
the higher offerings whose blood entered the holy place wholly burnt, not eaten (Leviticus 4:12)
a figure of the blood of reconciliation brought in by Christ and his suffering without the gate (Hebrews 9:12; 13:11-13; Colossians 1:20)
